Auction.brief: Represents an in-game auction event, managing item sale, participants, bids, and auction progression.
Auction.details: The Auction class encapsulates the lifecycle and state management of an in-game auction, including tracking the item being sold, the seller and buyer characters, the current highest bid, and the auction's status. It maintains timing control through pulse counters to regulate update intervals and enforces a minimum bid threshold to ensure fair bidding. The class provides methods for initializing and updating the auction state, as well as verifying participant involvement by object or character. Designed with copy constructor and assignment operator restrictions, it ensures data integrity and prevents unintended copying. The Auction class interacts with other core game components such as Object and Character to facilitate item transfer, bid handling, and auction flow, forming a vital part of the game's trading and economic systems.
Auction.notes: The class employs the singleton pattern for managing individual auction instances and restricts copying to maintain data consistency. The update method is intended to be called periodically, automating auction progression and notifications.
Auction.rationale: This class exists to centralize and manage the complex state transitions and interactions involved in in-game auctions, integrating with item and character systems to support a dynamic trading environment within the game architecture.
Auction.name: Auction

item.brief: Pointer to the Object currently being auctioned.
item.details: This variable references the Object that is actively involved in the current auction, representing the item up for bidding. It is initialized to nullptr when no auction is in progress, and assigned to a specific Object when an auction starts. Throughout the auction lifecycle, this pointer is used to access item details, display information to participants, and facilitate ownership transfer upon completion. It is consistently checked for nullptr to ensure that auction operations are only performed when an item is present, preventing invalid memory access. The Object class encapsulates the properties and behaviors of in-game items relevant to the auction system.
item.notes: The pointer is managed carefully to avoid dangling references; it is reset to nullptr when the auction concludes or is reset.
item.rationale: This variable exists to link the auction system with the specific item being sold, enabling dynamic interaction, display, and transfer of ownership during the auction process.
item.name: item

usages._auction_8cc, void do_auction(Character: Holds the object that the character intends to auction, after validation and removal from inventory.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::update(): Checked for nullptr at the start to determine if an auction is active; used to access item details for messages and transfer.
usages.class_auction, bool Auction::is_particip: Checked to ensure it is not null, serving as a prerequisite for the function's participant validation logic.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::init(): Reset to nullptr to indicate that no item is currently associated with the auction.
usages.class_auction, bool Auction::is_particip: Checked to ensure it is not null before comparing it to the object pointer, and then used in an equality comparison to determine participation.

seller.brief: Pointer to the Character who is currently selling the auctioned item.
seller.details: The 'seller' variable is a pointer to the Character object representing the individual who initiated or is responsible for the auction. It is initialized to nullptr when there is no active auction or after resetting the auction state. This pointer is essential for tracking ownership, verifying permissions, and managing transactions such as returning unsold items or awarding gold upon sale. It is used across various auction-related functions to identify the seller, enforce access controls, and maintain the relationship between the auction and the character involved in the process.
seller.notes: The 'seller' pointer may remain nullptr if no auction is active, indicating the absence of a current seller. Proper null checks are necessary before dereferencing to avoid potential errors.
seller.rationale: This variable exists to associate an auction with its initiating character, enabling the system to manage permissions, transactions, and state transitions accurately throughout the auction lifecycle.
seller.name: seller

usages._auction_8cc, void do_auction(Character: References the character who is selling the item, used to set auction ownership and for permission checks.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::update(): Used to identify the original owner of the item; involved in returning the item if unsold and in awarding gold upon sale.
usages.class_auction, bool Auction::is_particip: Compared with 'ch' to verify if 'ch' is the seller in the auction, influencing the return value.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::init(): Reset to nullptr to indicate that no seller is currently associated with the auction.

buyer.brief: Pointer to the Character currently participating as the highest bidder in the auction.
buyer.details: The 'buyer' variable stores a pointer to the Character object representing the current highest bidder in an ongoing auction. It is initialized to nullptr when there are no active bids, indicating that no participant has placed a bid yet. During the auction, this pointer is updated whenever a new bid surpasses the previous highest bid, ensuring accurate tracking of the leading bidder. It is used in functions such as 'do_auction' to record bids, 'update' to determine the winner, and 'is_participant' to verify if a character is the current buyer. Proper management of this pointer is essential for enforcing bidding rules, preventing self-bidding, and finalizing the sale process.
buyer.notes: The 'buyer' pointer is reset to nullptr at the start of each auction to indicate no active bids. It must be carefully updated to reflect the latest highest bidder and cleared when the auction concludes or is reset.
buyer.rationale: This variable exists to track the current highest bidder during an auction, enabling the system to manage bid comparisons, refunds, and sale finalization efficiently. It supports the auction's core functionality of identifying and rewarding the winning participant.
buyer.name: buyer

usages._auction_8cc, void do_auction(Character: Tracks the character who has placed the current highest bid, used to prevent self-bidding and handle refunds.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::update(): Used to identify the winning bidder; involved in transferring the item and in sale notifications.
usages.class_auction, bool Auction::is_particip: Compared with 'ch' to verify if 'ch' is the buyer in the auction, influencing the return value.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::init(): Reset to nullptr to indicate that no buyer is currently associated with the auction.

bet.brief: Stores the amount of the most recent bid in the auction.
bet.details: The 'bet' variable holds the value of the latest bid placed during the auction, initialized to zero to indicate no bids have been made yet. It is updated whenever a new, higher bid is received, serving as the current highest bid reference. This variable is essential for comparing incoming bids, managing the auction's bidding process, and providing real-time status updates to participants. Its proper management ensures the integrity of the bidding system and accurate auction progression.
bet.notes: The variable is typically reset or updated in response to new bids and is checked to determine if any bids have been placed. It is stored as an integer, reflecting the bid amount in the relevant currency or points system.
bet.rationale: The 'bet' variable exists to track the highest bid in the auction, enabling the system to enforce bid increments, determine the winner, and control the flow of the auction process effectively.
bet.name: bet

usages._auction_8cc, void do_auction(Character: Stores the current highest bid amount, used to compare against new bids and to display auction status.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::update(): Checked to determine if a bid has been received; used in messages and to process the sale or return of the item.

going.brief: An integer representing the current stage of the auction process.
going.details: The 'going' variable indicates the current phase of an ongoing auction, with specific values denoting different stages: 1 for the first call, 2 for the second call, and 3 indicating that the item has been sold. It is initialized to 0 when no auction is active, serving as a flag for auction status. This variable is updated throughout the auction lifecycle to track progress and is utilized in messaging functions and control flow to determine the current state of the auction process.
going.notes: The variable uses specific integer values to represent distinct auction stages, simplifying status checks. Its value is reset to 0 when no auction is in progress, ensuring clear state management.
going.rationale: The 'going' variable exists to facilitate tracking and managing the different phases of an auction, enabling the system to respond appropriately at each stage and ensuring correct auction flow and messaging.
going.name: going

usages._auction_8cc, void do_auction(Character: Indicates whether the auction is in progress or has been stopped, affecting control flow.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::update(): Incremented to track the auction stage (once, twice, sold); used in messages to indicate current auction status.

pulse.brief: Counter that tracks the number of 0.25-second intervals until the next auction call-out.
pulse.details: The 'pulse' variable serves as a countdown timer, representing the number of 0.25-second intervals remaining before the next auction-related event or update. It is initialized to zero and is decremented periodically, typically within the auction's update routines. When the counter reaches zero, it triggers the corresponding auction call-out or update, after which it is reset to control the pacing of auction activities. This mechanism ensures that auction events occur at consistent, timed intervals, maintaining proper synchronization with the system's timing cycle.
pulse.notes: The 'pulse' counter is often reset after reaching zero to schedule subsequent auction events, making it a key component for timing control within the auction system.
pulse.rationale: This variable exists to regulate the timing of auction call-outs, ensuring they occur at controlled intervals and do not overwhelm the system or users with too frequent updates.
pulse.name: pulse

usages._auction_8cc, void do_auction(Character: Timer that determines how long the auction remains active before concluding or resetting.
usages.class_auction, void Auction::update(): Decremented to manage timing; reset when reaching zero to control auction update intervals.

min.brief: The minimum bid amount permitted in the auction.
min.details: This variable holds the lowest bid value that participants can place during an auction, starting with an initial value of zero. It is set during auction initialization and is referenced throughout the bidding process to validate incoming bids, ensuring they meet or exceed this minimum threshold. The variable interacts with auction management functions to maintain bid integrity and enforce auction rules.
min.notes: The variable is typically stored as an integer and is reset or adjusted when auction parameters change. Proper validation against this value is crucial for consistent auction behavior.
min.rationale: The 'min' variable exists to define the lower limit for bids, preventing bids below a certain threshold and facilitating fair bidding practices within the auction system.
min.name: min

usages._auction_8cc, void do_auction(Character: Represents the minimum bid allowed for the auctioned item, validated during auction setup.

Auction::Auction().brief: Constructs a new Auction instance with default settings.
Auction::Auction().details: This constructor initializes an Auction object using default member initializations or default constructors for its members. As the implementation is empty, it does not perform any additional setup or resource allocation beyond what is specified by default member initializers or class member defaults. This allows for creating an Auction object in a default, unconfigured state, which can be customized later as needed.
Auction::Auction().notes: The constructor is trivial and relies on default member initializations, making object creation lightweight. It is suitable for scenarios where auction parameters will be set after construction.
Auction::Auction().rationale: Used when creating an Auction object that will be configured later, such as in testing or when default settings are sufficient initially.
Auction::Auction().name: Auction

virtual Auction::~Auction().brief: Virtual destructor for the Auction class to ensure proper cleanup of resources.
virtual Auction::~Auction().details: This destructor is declared virtual to support polymorphic deletion, allowing derived class destructors to be called correctly when deleting objects through base class pointers. Although the destructor body is empty, its virtual nature guarantees that any resources allocated by derived classes are properly released, maintaining resource safety and preventing leaks in inheritance hierarchies.
virtual Auction::~Auction().returns: This function does not return a value.
virtual Auction::~Auction().notes: Declaring the destructor virtual is crucial for classes intended to be inherited from, ensuring correct destruction order and resource management.
virtual Auction::~Auction().rationale: Used when deleting instances of derived classes through base class pointers, ensuring proper cleanup and avoiding resource leaks in polymorphic scenarios.
virtual Auction::~Auction().name: ~Auction

void Auction::init().brief: Resets the auction's internal state by clearing its item, buyer, and seller pointers.
void Auction::init().details: The init() function reinitializes the auction object by setting the item, buyer, and seller pointers to nullptr, effectively clearing any previous auction data. This prepares the auction instance for a new transaction or state, ensuring that no residual data from prior auctions remains. It is typically invoked during system startup or before starting a new auction to maintain data integrity and prevent unintended behavior caused by stale pointers.
void Auction::init().returns: This function does not return a value.
void Auction::init().notes: This function does not perform any memory deallocation; it only resets pointers to nullptr. Proper memory management should be handled elsewhere if needed.
void Auction::init().rationale: Used during system initialization or before starting a new auction to ensure the auction object is in a clean state, preventing data corruption or logic errors from previous auction data.
void Auction::init().name: init

usages.class_game, void Game::boot(): Called within the function to initialize the auction system, setting up its internal state before game operations proceed.

void Auction::update().brief: Updates the auction state by progressing stages, announcing bids, and finalizing or canceling the sale when the auction concludes.
void Auction::update().details: The function decreases the internal pulse counter; when it reaches zero, it resets the pulse to a predefined interval and advances the auction's 'going' state, which tracks whether the item is going once, twice, or has been sold. During each stage, it broadcasts appropriate messages to participants, indicating the current bid status or sale progress. When the auction reaches the 'sold' stage, it checks if there was a bid; if so, it transfers the item to the highest bidder, credits the seller with the bid amount, and sends notifications to all relevant parties, including immortal characters via wiznet. If no bids were received, it returns the item to the seller and announces the cancellation. The function relies on internal variables such as 'item', 'bet', 'buyer', 'seller', 'going', and 'pulse' to manage timing, state transitions, messaging, and finalization of the auction process, ensuring proper flow and communication throughout the auction lifecycle.

params.No: parameters; operates on internal auction state variables.

void Auction::update().returns: Void; performs side effects such as messaging, item transfer, and state updates without returning a value.
void Auction::update().notes: The function manages auction progression solely based on internal state variables and timing, ensuring synchronized updates and notifications. It handles both successful sales and cancellations gracefully, maintaining consistent game state and player communication.
void Auction::update().rationale: This function is used within the game's auction system to periodically update the auction status, announce current bids, and finalize or cancel sales, typically called at regular intervals to automate auction progression and ensure timely notifications and transactions.
void Auction::update().name: update

usages.update_8cc, void update_handler(): Called at the end of the function to process ongoing auction states, handle bids, and finalize sales or removals.

bool Auction::is_participant(Object *obj.brief: Checks whether the given object is the current item being auctioned.
bool Auction::is_participant(Object *obj.details: This function determines if the specified object is the active item in the ongoing auction. It first ensures that there is an item currently being auctioned by verifying that the item pointer is not null. Then, it compares the provided object pointer to the auctioned item pointer. If both conditions are true, it confirms that the object is indeed the current auctioned item, facilitating interactions such as display updates or ownership transfers related to the auction process.

params.obj: A pointer to the Object to be checked against the current auctioned item.

bool Auction::is_participant(Object *obj.returns: A boolean indicating whether the object is the current auctioned item.
bool Auction::is_participant(Object *obj.notes: This function is used internally to identify participation in the auction, such as preventing object decay or allowing specific interactions during the auction process.
bool Auction::is_participant(Object *obj.rationale: Used in scenarios where the system needs to verify if an object involved in the auction is the one currently being sold, such as during updates or validation checks in auction-related workflows.
bool Auction::is_participant(Object *obj.name: is_participant

usages.act__comm_8cc, void do_quit(Character *c: Called to check if the character is currently participating in an auction, preventing quitting during an auction.
usages.update_8cc, void obj_update(): Used to skip decay processing for objects involved in an auction, preventing their removal during auction activities.

bool Auction::is_participant(Character *.brief: Checks whether a given character is currently participating in the auction as either the buyer or seller.
bool Auction::is_participant(Character *.details: This function determines if the specified character is actively involved in the current auction by verifying that there is an item being auctioned and that the character pointer is valid. It then compares the character to the stored buyer and seller pointers associated with the auction. If the character matches either the buyer or the seller, the function returns true, indicating participation; otherwise, it returns false. This check is useful for validating whether a character is directly engaged in the ongoing auction process.

params.ch: Pointer to the Character object to verify for participation in the auction.

bool Auction::is_participant(Character *.returns: A boolean indicating whether the character is the current buyer or seller in the auction.
bool Auction::is_participant(Character *.notes: The function assumes that the auction's item, buyer, and seller pointers are properly managed and updated elsewhere in the system. It performs null checks to prevent invalid memory access.
bool Auction::is_participant(Character *.rationale: This method is used in auction workflows to confirm if a character is involved in the current auction, which can influence permissions, display logic, or bid validation in the game system.
bool Auction::is_participant(Character *.name: is_participant

Auction::Auction(const Auction &).brief: Copy constructor for the Auction class.
Auction::Auction(const Auction &).details: Creates a new instance of the Auction class by copying the state of an existing Auction object. This constructor ensures that the new object is an exact replica of the original, maintaining all its properties and current state.
Auction::Auction(const Auction &).returns: This constructor does not return a value as it is used to initialize a new object.
Auction::Auction(const Auction &).name: Auction

Auction & Auction::operator=(const Aucti.brief: Assigns the values from another Auction object to this one.
Auction & Auction::operator=(const Aucti.details: This copy assignment operator takes another Auction object as a parameter and copies its contents into the current object. It ensures that the current object becomes a duplicate of the provided Auction object, handling any necessary cleanup of existing resources and allocation of new resources as needed.

params.other: The Auction object whose values are to be copied.

Auction & Auction::operator=(const Aucti.returns: A reference to the current Auction object after assignment.
Auction & Auction::operator=(const Aucti.name: operator=
